coredata.dc:title: 2D/2D heterojunction of graphitic carbon nitride and hexagonal boron nitride nanosheets mediated electrochemical detection of hazardous hydroquinone with high selectivity and sensitivity
coredata.dc:creator: Harikrishnan, K.
Singh, Gajendar
Kushwaha, Amisha
Singh, Varun Pratap
Gaur, Umesh Kumar
Sharma, Manu
coredata.dc:description: The presence of organic pollutants in our environment is often unnoticed and they silently create serious health hazards. Monitoring and control of these harmful chemicals in both land and water is very much necessary. Graphitic carbon nitride/boron nitride (g-C3N4/BN) nanocomposites were successfully synthesized through a solvothermal process and characterized via XRD, FTIR, UV-Visible, SEM, TEM, and XPS techniques. The electrochemical behavior of g-C3N4/BN nanocomposites was studied using Cyclic Voltammetry (CV), impedance potential, and A.C – impedance experiments. In this work, g-C3N4/BN, 2D‐2D nanocomposite-based electrochemical sensor was developed to detect hazardous hydroquinone (HydQ). CV, Differential Pulse Voltammetry (DPV), and amperometric detection techniques were employed for the detection of HydQ and achieved a good limit of detection of 8.2 nM. g-C3N4/BN(1:1)@GCE exhibits a lower flat band potential of −2.15 V and charge transfer resistance (Rct) of 375.0 Ω than other ratios of g-C3N4/BN nanocomposites based electrodes, making it most suitable for sensor application. The practicality of the prepared electrochemical sensor was validated by performing real sample analysis using industrial wastewater and tap water as analytes.

head.ce:title: 2D/2D heterojunction of graphitic carbon nitride and hexagonal boron nitride nanosheets mediated electrochemical detection of hazardous hydroquinone with high selectivity and sensitivity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: VP & RPTP Science College, Vallabh Vidya Nagar, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: VP & RPTP Science College, Vallabh Vidya Nagar, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The presence of organic pollutants in our environment is often unnoticed and they silently create serious health hazards. Monitoring and control of these harmful chemicals in both land and water is very much necessary. Graphitic carbon nitride/boron nitride (g-C3N4/BN) nanocomposites were successfully synthesized through a solvothermal process and characterized via XRD, FTIR, UV-Visible, SEM, TEM, and XPS techniques. The electrochemical behavior of g-C3N4/BN nanocomposites was studied using Cyclic Voltammetry (CV), impedance potential, and A.C – impedance experiments. In this work, g-C3N4/BN, 2D‐2D nanocomposite-based electrochemical sensor was developed to detect hazardous hydroquinone (HydQ). CV, Differential Pulse Voltammetry (DPV), and amperometric detection techniques were employed for the detection of HydQ and achieved a good limit of detection of 8.2 nM. g-C3N4/BN(1:1)@GCE exhibits a lower flat band potential of −2.15 V and charge transfer resistance (Rct) of 375.0 Ω than other ratios of g-C3N4/BN nanocomposites based electrodes, making it most suitable for sensor application. The practicality of the prepared electrochemical sensor was validated by performing real sample analysis using industrial wastewater and tap water as analytes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The presence of hazardous molecules in the environment in excess amounts is a serious threat to human health as well as to the flora and fauna [1–4]. Hazardous molecules are released to the environment by various means like refuse from industries, waste products thrown out by people, pesticides used in agricultural fields, etc [5,6]. The silent pollution caused by industries is often left unnoticed and most of them do not have the right technology to filter out hazardous molecules before sewage discharge. [7]. Hydroquinone (HydQ), also known as 1,4-Benzenediol is a white-colored water-soluble phenolic compound that is widely used as a skin lightening agent, antioxidant, photosensitizer, pharmaceutical reagent, ingredient of pesticides, and in the manufacturing of dyes and plastics [8,9]. HydQ has proven to hinder melanogenesis (melanocyte cells producing melanin pigments)\both in vivo and in vitro and most of the HydQ based depigmentation cream formulations contain 2–5% of HydQ [10,11]. HydQ is an established carcinogenic and haematotoxic agent which has been already banned or restricted its use by many countries [12]. HydQ is considered officially an environmental pollutant in European Union (EU), Japan, and the United States (US). HydQ can enter the human body through mouth, nose, or skin and can cause adverse health effects such as skin and mucosa erosion, inhibition of the nervous system, etc. Also, with higher concentrations, it can cause serious health issues like tachycardia, kidney failure, severe headache, decompensation, fatigue, and can even cause death [13]. The degradation of HydQ is difficult and it is also very toxic to aquatic organisms [14,15]. So, it is extremely essential to create a rapid, reliable, and economical sensor for detecting hazardous HydQ in the surroundings. Currently, HydQ is detected using complicated, expensive, and time taking techniques such as fluorescence spectroscopy, HPLC, GC-MS, colorimetric sensing, capillary electrophoresis, and surface enhanced Raman scattering [16–21]. In recent years, electrochemical sensing techniques have attracted researchers due to its high speed, sensitivity, selectivity, ease of operation, and good limit of detection [22,23]. All these exceptional characteristics of electrochemical sensors mainly depend upon materials chosen for electrode preparation. 2D nanosheets having high surface area have attracted researchers for sensing applications. Environment-friendly alkali-free techniques are now available for 2D nanosheet synthesis [24].

Researchers have reported different composites based on graphene and graphene oxide with various organic compounds and metal oxides that show good electrochemical sensitivity for HydQ [25–28]. Graphene quantum dots functionalized metal oxides are also been reported for the electrochemical sensing application of HydQ [29,30]. Yin and co-workers explored the electrochemical detection potential of CoFe2Se4 implanted porous carbon nanofibers for simultaneous detection of HydQ, resorcinol, and catechol [31]. Peng et al. reported the use of MoS2/r-GO nanocomposites for sensing HydQ[32]. Recently, Raymundo-Pereira et al. developed cost-effective carbon screen printed electrodes for the detection of HydQ, estradiol, and paracetamol simultaneously in tap water [33]. Still, there is a need for an eco-friendly, cost-effective, and highly sensitive sensor for detecting the presence of HydQ in our environment quickly and accurately.

Remarkably, porous two-dimensional graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) possesses a high surface area and good electrical conductivity because of its sheet-like structure. g-C3N4 can act as a good supporting material for various nanostructures and synergistically help them in fast charge transport [34–37]. These exceptional features of g-C3N4 improve the electrochemical exchanges between electrolyte and electrode.

Boron nitride (BN), also known as white graphene, has a honeycomb-like atomic structure [38]. BN is known for its broad band gap (5.9 eV), good thermal stability, high surface area, chemical inertness, resistance to oxidation, high melting point (2973 ℃), photoconductivity, and nontoxicity [39,40]. Among various crystal structures of BN, hexagonal boron nitride (h-BN) is the most stable form [41]. Considering these factors, we have developed g-C3N4/BN nanocomposites for electrochemical sensing of HydQ, since both g-C3N4 and BN have 2D structures and the facial-facial interaction happens between g-C3N4 and BN nanostructures. The use of g-C3N4 and BN has got many unique advantages in electrochemical applications because of their 2D/2D interaction that helps in fast charge transfer. Low intrinsic resistance and small charge transport distance between both the materials lead to stable oxidation potentials in electrochemical reactions [42,43]. To the best of our information, this is the first to report on the application of g-C3N4/BN nanocomposite for electrochemical sensing of HydQ.

In this work, g-C3N4 has been synthesized by thermal polycondensation of thiourea at 550 ℃, and g-C3N4 sheets with folds-like morphology were observed by TEM analysis. Bulk BN was ultrasonically treated to induce individual sheet separation for obtaining thin BN nanosheets. The g-C3N4/BN nanocomposites were synthesized by a simple solvothermal route. g-C3N4/BN(1:1) nanocomposite showed the best electrochemical performance in CV and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) analysis, and it has been used for HydQ sensing. The limit of detection and linear range of recently reported sensors for HydQ detection was significant compared with this work ( Table 1). The robustness of the sensor was proven by conducting selectivity, stability, and reusability tests. Furthermore, the practicality of the sensor has been validated using tap water and industrial wastewater samples.

Analytical grade thiourea (CH4N2S), boron nitride (BN) powder (98% pure), ethanol (99.9%), potassium ferricyanide [K3(Fe(CN)6)], potassium ferrocyanide [K4(Fe(CN)6)], potassium chloride (KCl), sodium phosphate monobasic dihydrate (NaH2PO4·2H2O), sodium phosphate dibasic anhydrous (Na2HPO4), and hydroquinone (C6H4(OH)2) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and used exclusively of any additional purifications. Glucose, caffeine, serotonin, dopamine, theophylline, ascorbic acid, and hydrogen peroxide were additionally used for selectivity analysis. Double-distilled water was used to prepare all the reagents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of g-C3N4 and preparation of BN nanosheets
ce:section.ce:para: Thermal polycondensation route was used for the synthesis of g-C3N4 from thiourea [44,45]. In brief, 6 g of analytical grade thiourea was taken in a glass crucible and was heat treated inside a muffle furnace at 550 ℃ for 4 h. Thus, obtained light yellow product was grounded well using mortar and pestle to form a fine powder. It was then measured for yield and stored for further characterization. In 200 ml of distilled water, 200 mg of bulk boron nitride (BN) powder was added and subjected to sonication for 8 cycles of 1 h duration each with a holding time of 30 min between each cycle. Sonication treatment will facilitate individual sheet separation from bulk boron nitride particles [46]. The milky-white solution was obtained after sonication treatment and centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 5 min allowing big particles of boron nitride to settle down. The supernatant was carefully transferred to a new centrifuge tube and again centrifuged at 5000 rpm for five minutes to filter out the remaining small-sized bulk particles of boron nitride. After this, the supernatant was centrifuged for five minutes at 8000 rpm to allow fine nanosheets of boron nitride to settle at the wall of the centrifuge tube. The supernatant was discarded, and the sample was diluted with distilled water and sonicated for 1 h in a bath sonicator for proper dispersal. This mixture was then poured into a petri dish and then dried inside a hot air oven overnight at 60 ℃ for obtaining nanosheets of BN. All the bulk boron nitride particles which were obtained during each of the centrifuge cycles as waste product was collected and kept for reusing by providing further sonication treatment to allow better sheet separation.

Solvothermal route was applied for the synthesis of gC3N4/BN nanocomposites. Equal weights of BN and g-C3N4 (10 mg each) were added to a solution containing 40 ml of 50% ethanol (1:1; ethanol: water) and was sonicated in a bath sonicator for 1 h. This highly dispersed solution was transferred to an autoclave. It was then kept inside a hot air oven at 180 ℃ for 10 h. After cooling down the autoclave at room temperature, the sample was centrifuged, washed with water and ethanol, and transferred to a petri dish. This was then dried inside a hot air oven overnight to acquire fine light yellowish powder. Two different ratios of g-C3N4/BN (1:2 and 2:1) were also prepared following the same procedure.

X-ray diffraction was executed with Panalytical X pert Pro coupled XRD with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å) to examine the crystalline phases of g-C3N4, BN, and g-C3N4/BN nanocomposites. Fourier transform infrared spectra were obtained from Perkin Elmer, Spectrum 65 FT-IR Spectrometer utilizing KBr as the standard of transmission for the analysis of functional groups present. UV-Visible absorption studies were done from 200 to 800 nm wavelength using Shimadzu UV-1800 UV spectrophotometer using double distilled water as a diluting agent. Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopic studies were carried out using Quanta 200 FEG SEM. Transmission Electron Microscopic images were captured using JEOL JEM 2100 TEM HR LaB6 Version High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope. The XPS spectrum was recorded using an omicron nanotechnology XPS instrument. Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) analysis was done for surface area analysis using Quantachrome instruments NOVAtouch® LX2 rapid two sample surface area and pore size analyzer.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV), Mott-Schottky (MS), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) studies were examined using CHI 920D scanning electrochemical microscope (SECM). 5 mM K3[Fe(CN)6]/ K4[Fe(CN)6] made in 0.1 M KCl solution was used as electrolyte for electrode optimization studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of electrode and electrochemical detection of HydQ
ce:section.ce:para: For constructing the working electrode, a 3 mm glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was polished with aluminum slurry on paper to obtain a mirror finish, it was then sonicated in a blend of distilled water and ethanol (1:1) and dried well before use. In 300 ml of distilled water, 3 mg of sample was dispersed and ultrasonicated for 5 min to attain uniform distribution in the solution and this solution was used to coat the polished electrode surface using the drop-casting method. HydQ was electrochemically detected using CV, DPV, and amperometric (i-t) techniques. The electrochemical detection experiments were done using a three-electrode system with the platinum rod as counter electrode and Ag/AgCl electrode as reference electrode. pH 7 PBS buffer was used as electrolyte for all detection studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sample preparation for real sample analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The industrial effluent was obtained from an industrial region in Ahmedabad and municipal tap water was sourced from a local tap. Initially, the wastewater was centrifuged at 7000 rpm to separate solid particles and the supernatant was diluted in pH 7 PBS prior to sensing. For the analysis of the tap water sample, pH 7 PBS buffer was made entirely from the collected tap water itself and employed without any further modification. Each experiment was repeated two more times for result validation. DPV and amperometric (i-t) methods were employed for real sample analysis (for the detection of HydQ under optimum environments).

Fig. 1(a) displays the XRD patterns of pure g-C3N4, BN, and g-C3N4/BN nanocomposites. Diffraction peaks of g-C3N4 were observed at 2θ values of 12.79° and 27.83° which are assigned to crystal planes (100) and (002) respectively, corresponding to the interplanar stacking and interlayer packaging separately [47]. It matches with JCPDS card No: 87–1526. The PXRD Characteristic peaks of BN were found at 2θ values of nearly 26.75°, 41.69°, 44.02°, 55.12°, and 75.94° which are matched with the planes (002), (100), (101), (004) and (110) respectively with hexagonal boron nitride (h-BN) crystal structure (JCPDS Card No. 34–0421). In all the composites, each diffraction peak of both g-C3N4 and BN was found with reduced intensity. Strong peaks in the 2θ region of 25° to 30° are magnified and presented in Fig. 1(a) showing the occurrence of both the peaks of g-C3N4 and BN in composites. A minor BN peak shifting can be observed in composites which can be due to the intermolecular interaction between the layered structures of BN with g-C3N4. Information about the functional groups present in the sample was identified by performing FTIR spectroscopy [48]. In Fig. 1(b), a strong band of g-C3N4 seen at 810 cm−1 is the standard triazine-based units’ mode of breathing [49]. Stretching modes in the section 1200–1650 cm−1 are specific for C-N and CN in heterocycles [50]. Stretches of absorption at 1638 cm−1 and 1574 cm−1 are due to CN stretching modes and aromatic C-N stretching is shown by the bands at 1460, 1407, 1316, and 1237 cm−1. Also, wide absorption bands present in the area 3000 – 3300 cm−1 related to different vibration modes of OH and NH bonds of uncondensed amino groups [51]. In the graph of BN, the peak at 804 cm−1 represents the B-N-B bond out of plane bending vibrations [52]. B-N in-plane stretching vibration is denoted by the peak present at 1385 cm−1 [53]. In gC3N4/BN, all the peaks present in gC3N4 are visible but with less intensity. The light absorption capability of g-C3N4, BN, and g-C3N4/BN nanocomposites was explored from UV-Visible spectra presented in Fig. 1(c). Maximum absorption of light was shown at 206 nm and 318 nm by BN and gC3N4 respectively. The plot of g-C3N4/BN nanocomposites encompassed peaks from gC3N4 in the region 370 nm and 389 nm. Also, a broad peak at the region of 326 nm subsequent to the broad peak at 318 nm in g-C3N4 can be noticed in the graph principally suggesting the formation of g-C3N4/BN nanocomposite. The optical band gap of samples was found using the Tauc plot method from the optical absorption data obtained from UV-Visible spectra and is represented in Fig. 1(d). The optical band gap of g-C3N4, BN, g-C3N4/BN(1:1), g-C3N4/BN(2:1), and g-C3N4/BN(1:2) was found to be 2.80 eV, 5.30 eV, 2.25 eV, 2.47 eV, and 2.26 eV respectively. After the addition of BN in g-C3N4 samples, the band gap of composites decreased from 2.80 eV in bare g-C3N4 to 2.25 eV in g-C3N4/BN(1:1), which suggests good interaction of BN with g-C3N4. The lowest band gap energy shown by the g-C3N4/BN(1:1) composite may be because of some changes in structure and morphology or due to good interaction between these materials at an optimal ratio. The morphology of the selective composition obtained after solvothermal synthesis at 180 °C was studied using Scanning Electron Microscope ( Fig. 2). The interaction of boron nitride with graphitic carbon nitride was ensured by the scanning electron micrographs. A close observation of the micrographs discloses the coarse surface of g-C3N4 formed after high temperature and high-pressure solvothermal synthesis procedure which may provide a reactive surface for good BN interaction. Small flakes of BN sheets were seen to be intermingled with g-C3N4 layers and these layers were found to be stacked together having sections of BN sheets sandwiched between them. The HR-TEM photographs of g-C3N4, BN nanosheets, and g-C3N4/BN(1:1) nanocomposite were examined. In Fig. 3(a), the thin sheet-like morphology of g-C3N4 was observed and close observation of individual sheets reveals the slightly folded sheets. In Fig. 3(b) the stacking of boron nitride nanosheets was observed. The g-C3N4/BN(1:1) nanocomposite clearly shows the 2D‐2D interaction between the sheets of g-C3N4 and BN (Fig. 3(c)). The SAED pattern of g-C3N4/BN(1:1) nanocomposite is shown in Fig. 3(d) and suggests less crystallinity of the composite. The interaction of BN with g-C3N4 has created closed interfaces that can enhance the electrochemical sensing ability of g-C3N4/BN nanocomposite.

X-Ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) was executed to investigate the surface elemental characteristics as well as the oxidation states of g-C3N4/BN nanocomposite and to study the interaction among graphitic carbon nitride and boron nitride in g-C3N4/BN(1:1) nanocomposite. From the full survey spectra of g-C3N4/BN nanocomposite ( Fig. 4(a)), it showed only the presence of carbon, nitrogen, boron, and oxygen. At 531 eV, the occurrence of a feeble O 1 s peak could be due to the absorption of carbon dioxide or water molecules by the sample [54]. As indicated in Fig. 4(b) of C 1 s high-resolution spectra, the peak at 288.4 eV is assigned to N-CN and 285 eV is attributed to C-C [55,56]. In Fig. 4(c), the B 1 s spectra show binding energy of 190.7 eV which is allocated to B-N, and binding energy of 191.5 eV is designated to B-N-C moieties [57,58]. In Fig. 4(d), the spectra of N 1 s show a peak located at 398.2 eV, which is associated with the binding energy of sp2 hybridized N-B and the peak located at 399.2 eV corresponds to C-N-B moieties which may be responsible for enhanced electrochemical performance displayed by the g-C3N4/BN(1:1) nanocomposite [59,60].

The specific surface area of the best sample, g-C3N4/BN(1:1), using Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) analysis has also been obtained. The results are shown in Fig. 4(e) and 4 (f). The specific surface area of the g-C3N4/BN(1:1) composite was found to be 37.66 m²/g. The pore volume and pore radius were found to be 0.34 cc/g and 14.05 nm respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characteristics of g-C3N4, BN, and g-C3N4/BN nanocomposites modified GCE
ce:section.ce:para: Cyclic voltammetric measurements were studied at a scan rate of 10–100 mVs−1 for each sample. CV plots of g-C3N4 @GCE and BN@GCE in Fig. 5(a) and (c) correspondingly shows the redox peaks formed with applied anodic and cathodic currents respectively. Fig. 5(b) and (d) shows the relationship of the cathodic and anodic peak current to the square root of scan rates for g-C3N4 as well as BN modified GCE respectively. Similarly, Fig. 6(a), (c) and (e) shows the CV plots of the nanocomposites of g-C3N4and BN in the ratio 1:1, 2:1, and 1:2 respectively, determining the influence of scan rates over peak currents and potentials related to them. Higher peak currents were observed in g-C3N4/BN(1:1) nanocomposite when compared with the CV plots of bare samples of g-C3N4 and BN indicating increased electron transfer between electrode and electrolyte. The peak related to the cathodic current was found to shift to the negative side and the anodic current peak was found to shift to the positive side of potentials with increasing scan rate. Fig. 6(b), (d), and (f) show the linear dependence of redox peak currents over the scan rate square roots for g-C3N4/BN(1:1)@GCE, g-C3N4/BN(2:1)@GCE, and g-C3N4/BN(1:1)@GCE respectively, suggesting an interaction between g-C3N4 and BN nanosheets.

The active surface area for g-C3N4/BN(1:1)@GCE was calculated using the Randles-Sevcik equation:
Ip = 2·69 ×10
5
n
3/2
AD
1/2
CV
1/2 (at room temperature, 25 ℃)
Where Ip is the maximum current, n corresponds to the sum of electrons transferred, A represents the active surface area of modified GCE, D stands for the diffusion coefficient of electrolyte used, C represents the concentration of electrolyte in bulk, and V represents the scan rate. Both Ipa and Ipc peak currents for g-C3N4/BN nanocomposites modified GCE was found to be proportional to the scan rates square roots as presented in Fig. 6(b), (d), and (f). The active surface area of g-C3N4/BN(1:1)@GCE was found to be 0.466 cm2 with the aid of n, D, and C parameters in the Randles-Sevcik equation.

The impedance potential (IMPE) characteristics of g-C3N4 and BN modified GCE in the electrolyte containing 5 mM K3[Fe(CN)6]/ K4[Fe(CN)6] in 0.1 M KCl solution is shown in Fig. 7(a). Similarly, the IMPE characteristics of g-C3N4/BN nanocomposites in the ratio 1:1, 2:1, and 1:2 modified GCE are displayed in Fig. 7(b). The flat band potential of g-C3N4@GCE was found to be − 0.55 V and for BN@GCE it was found to be − 1.15 V as given in Fig. 7(a). As indicated in Fig. 7(b), the flat band potentials of g-C3N4/BN(1:1)@GCE, g-C3N4/BN(2:1)@GCE, and g-C3N4/BN(1:2)@GCE were found to be − 2.15, − 1.99 and − 2.21 respectively.

The optical band gap data obtained from the Tauc plot (Fig. 1(d)) and the flat band potential found from Mott-Schottky plots (Fig. 7(a) and 7 (b)) were used to construct the band position of g-C3N4, BN, and g-C3N4/BN nanocomposites as shown in Fig. 7(c).

Fig. 7(d) shows the EIS Nyquist plot of g-C3N4, BN, and g-C3N4/BN nanocomposites. In Fig. 7(d), g-C3N4/BN(1:1)@GCE retains the hemisphere with a smaller radius (charge transfer resistance, Rct value) than other samples and thereby suggesting having a lesser resistance which in turn will help in effective transportation of charge carriers between electrode and electrolyte. The Rct value for g-C3N4@GCE, BN@GCE, g-C3N4/BN(1:1)@GCE, C3N4/BN(2:1)@GCE, and C3N4/BN(1:2)@GCE were found to be 440.4 Ω, 774.5 Ω, 375.0 Ω, 480.4 Ω, and 724.5 Ω respectively.

CV, DPV, and amperometric (i-t) methods were employed for the electrochemical detection of HydQ. The effect of pH and the amount of catalyst loaded on GCE for HydQ detection were also studied to find out the optimum parameters for electrochemical sensing of HydQ. From the electrochemical studies, the highest performance was achieved by g-C3N4/BN(1:1)@GCE, so it was used for further electrochemical sensing studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The influence of catalyst loading and pH on HydQ oxidation
ce:section.ce:para: The amount of catalyst used for coating the GCE surface and its effect on electrochemical sensing of HydQ was studied using CV analysis utilizing 0.3 μM HydQ. The peak oxidation and reduction current of HydQ was increased from 2 μL to 6 μL of loaded catalyst but the peak current was found reduced for 7 μL loaded electrode as shown in Fig. 8(a). Since the highest current was observed for 6 μL catalyst loading, which may be providing the optimum thickness while drop casting was considered for further studies. Fig. 8(b) shows the dependence of reduction and oxidation current over loaded-catalyst.

The influence of pH on sensing HydQ electrochemically was studied by means of CV and DPV techniques employing 0.3 μM HydQ solution and is shown in Fig. 8(c) and (e) respectively. PBS buffer having pH 5, 7, 9, and 11 was used for the study and it revealed that the oxidation current of HydQ raised from pH 5 to pH 7 and then came down with the increase in pH to 11 in both CV and DPV analysis. Thus, the highest oxidation current for HydQ was obtained at pH 7 which may be due to the protic aromatic nature of HydQ having a pKa value of 9.9. An increase in the peak current from pH 3–7 is a result of protonation of the compound forming –OH2 + at lower pH values. Once the pH increases from 7, the hydroxyl ion concentration gets increased that in turn blocks HydQ to get absorbed onto the surface of g- g-C3N4/BN(1:1)@GCE, and as a result, with increasing pH from 7 to 11, the peak current decreases. The π-π interaction between the surface of g-C3N4/BN(1:1)@GCE and the aromatic ring of HydQ gets disturbed at lower pH due to the protonation of the electrode surface. The water clusters formed at these centers bring together phosphates from the electrolyte and resist the adsorption of HydQ.

The reliance of oxidation and reduction potential as well as oxidation and reduction current on pH in the case of CV analysis is given in Fig. 8(d). The dependency of buffer pH on the oxidation of HydQ can be denoted as a linear function Epa (V) = −0.02 (pH) + 0.1161 with coefficient of regression, R2 = 0.9961 and the dependence of buffer pH on the reduction of HydQ can be denoted as a linear function Epc (V) = −0.046 (pH) + 0.5583 with regression coefficient, R2 = 0.7615. Buffer with pH 7 was thus fixed for HydQ detection. The reliance of oxidation potential and oxidation current on pH in DPV analysis is given in Fig. 8(f). It can be noticed from the figure that, with the increase in pH from 7 to 11, the oxidation peaks of HydQ got shifted to negative potential values. The dependence of buffer pH on the oxidation of HydQ can be denoted as a linear function Epa (V) = −0.032 (pH) + 0.2697 with coefficient of regression, R2 = 0.9818.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of scan rate over the oxidation of HydQ
ce:section.ce:para:
For studying the effect of scan rate over HydQ oxidation, the CV technique was employed. PBS buffer of pH 7 was used as an electrolyte during the analysis. The reduction peak for HydQ was recorded at − 0.03 V and the oxidation peak for HydQ was noted at 0.15 V. The rate of the scan was varied from 10 mVs−1 to 100 mVs−1 and the reduction and oxidation peaks were found to increment accordingly with increasing scan rate. Fig. 9(a) represents the cyclic voltammogram of HydQ sensing and Fig. 9(b) shows the relationship of cathodic and anodic peak current to the scan rate square roots for g-C3N4/BN(1:1)@GCE. The equation for regression for HydQ reduction current can be written as Ipc (μA) = 3.22 (scan rate 1/2) - 0.4807 with the coefficient of regression, R2 = 0.9991 and the equation for regression for HydQ reduction current can be written as Ipa (μA) = −4.89 (scan rate 1/2) - 2.8264 with the coefficient of regression, R2 = 0.9993. Moreover, the differential pulse voltammetry technique was also employed for accomplishing improved sensitivity for HydQ detection. DPV technique eliminates non-faradaic type current and leads to the generation of better signals when compared with CV [61]. Fig. 9(c) shows HydQ’s DPV response recorded in pH 7 PBS buffer (0.1 M) on g-C3N4/BN(1:1) modified glassy carbon electrode with varying concentrations of HydQ from 0.02 to 0.16 μM. The scan rate was set at 50 mAs−1. The peak current of oxidation (Ipa) recorded continuous increment with adding HydQ concentration from 0.02 to 0.16 μM. Current vs HydQ concentration plot is shown in Fig. 9(d) in which the linearity was recorded from 0.02 μM to 0.09 μM with the regression equation Ipa = −194.26 (HydQ) - 25.26 with coefficient of regression, R2 = 0.9988 and from 0.10 μM to 0.16 μM with regression equation Ipa = −300.35 (HydQ) - 15.08 having coefficient of regression, R2 = 0.9963. The sensing range of HydQ was from 0.02 μM to 0.16 μM and electrochemical LOD was calculated as 0.0082 μM using the formula:
LOD =
3 × σ
s
Where σ is the standard deviation and s symbolizes the slope of the calibration plot. Interestingly, the LOD of HydQ was found relatively better than other recent reports as depicted in Table 1.

Further, HydQ was detected using the amperometric (i-t) detection technique. The current response of HydQ, which was added sequentially at an optimum potential of 0.05 V is displayed in Fig. 9(e). For detecting HydQ, the amperometric scan was carried out for 600 s and after reaching a saturated current, HydQ was successively added. Time taken for HydQ to get oxidized was found to be around 2 s from the amperometric current responses and thereby suggesting quick transport of charge between the surface of g-C3N4/BN(1:1)@GCE and electrolyte which may be because of adsorption of HydQ molecules on to the surface of g-C3N4/BN(1:1)@GCE. Fig. 9(f) denotes the reliance of oxidation current on consecutively added HydQ. Regression equation was satisfied by the amperometric linear current response: Ip (μA) = −1.802 (HydQ, μM) – 0.115 with R2 = 0.95032 from 0.02 to 0.08 μM and from 0.09 to 0.17 μM, the equation for regression: Ip (μA) = −4.468 (HydQ, μM) – 0.112 with R2 = 099921. The LOD was found to be 9.23 nM. The quick response and high sensitivity of HydQ by amperometric sensing proved the efficacy of g-C3N4/BN(1:1)@GCE as a HydQ sensor.

Specificity for amperometric HydQ sensing was tested using the amperometric (i-t) technique by adding glucose, caffeine, serotonin, dopamine, theophylline, ascorbic acid, and hydrogen peroxide along with HydQ to the reaction system. As shown in Fig. 10, at the applied voltage of 0.05 V, the current response for amperometric oxidation was only provided by HydQ. Other materials added to the reaction system did not produce any notable difference in the amperometric current and this proves that the sensor is highly selective for HydQ only and can efficiently perform in the presence of a consortium of analytes and can be used for practical real-sample analysis.

The presence of HydQ was investigated in 2 real samples: (1) Industrial wastewater collected from Ahmedabad, India, and (2) Tap water from Gandhinagar, India, for examining the real-sample usefulness of the developed sensor. The standard spiking method was used in the DPV technique for detecting HydQ in real samples. The sample preparation procedure followed is provided in the experimental section. The DPV responses of industrial wastewater sample and tap water sample before and after HydQ spiking is presented in Fig. 11 (a) and 11 (c) respectively. Since HydQ was not found in the collected industrial wastewater, the spiking technique was followed for sensor validation. HydQ was successively added to the industrial wastewater sample by 0.1, 0.12, and 0.14 μM, to make sure to get spikes in the DPV response graph for confirming the workability of developed g-C3N4/BN(1:1)@GCE. The relationship between the amount of HydQ spiked and oxidation current of HydQ for industrial wastewater and tap water is given in Fig. 11 (b) and 11 (d) respectively. 96.42 – 99% recoveries for HydQ spiked in industrial wastewater sample was attained. The relative standard deviation was in the range of 2.35–3.62% ( Table 2). Likewise, in the tap water sample also, the presence of HydQ was not detected and the spiking method was followed in the same way before. 97 – 98.57% recoveries of HydQ were found after spiking with a relative standard deviation between 2.48% and 3.22%. These remarkable values obtained for relative standard deviation and recoveries confirm that g-C3N4/BN(1:1)@GCE is an appropriate detector for the sensing of HydQ in the real sample with a good range up to nanomolar levels.

Long-term storage stability of g-C3N4/BN(1:1)@GCE was studied by CV analysis using 0.3 μM HydQ. The CV response using g-C3N4/BN(1:1)@GCE was recorded under pre-optimized conditions after storing it for 5, 10, 15, 20, and 25 days as shown in Fig. 11 (e) with inset representing the dependence of HdyQ oxidation and reduction current over the storage period. There was no major difference noticed in the detection efficiency of g-C3N4/BN(1:1)@GCE despite the fact that it has been stored for 25 days.

The reusability of g-C3N4/BN(1:1)@GCE was tested by performing CV analysis for detecting HydQ at pH 7 in PBS buffer 5 times repeatedly. After finishing each analysis, the electrode was gently taken out of the cell and dried inside a hot air oven before using it for performing the next cycle. Fig. 11 (f) shows the CV plot of g-C3N4/BN(1:1)@GCE recyclability assay against 0.3 μM HydQ for 5 repeated cycles with inset showing the dependency of HydQ oxidation and reduction current over repeated cycles. Only a minute reduction in current was observed in the CV plot after each cycle. The results indicate that the g-C3N4/BN(1:1)@GCE based sensor for HydQ detection can be used multiple times efficiently making it a more cost-effective sensor.

In the present work, ultrasonication and solvothermal methods are employed for the synthesis of g-C3N4/BN(1:1), g-C3N4/BN(2:1), and g-C3N4/BN(1:2) nanocomposites. On the basis of optical and electrochemical properties, g-C3N4/BN(1:1) nanocomposite was found superior to other nanocomposites. Therefore, g-C3N4/BN(1:1)@GCE has been employed for the electrochemical detection of HydQ. DPV analysis of HydQ demonstrated two linear ranges 0.02–0.09 μM and 0.10–0.16 μM. The detection limit was found to be 0.0082 μM. The amperometric method demonstrated linear ranges 0.02–0.08 μM and 0.09–0.17 μM with a limit of detection of 0.0092 μM. Moreover, the selectivity of the sensor for HydQ detection was studied using the amperometric (i-t) technique against glucose, caffeine, serotonin, dopamine, theophylline, ascorbic acid, and hydrogen peroxide interferences and showed that oxidation current response was only produced by HydQ proving that the sensor is highly selective towards HydQ. Real sample analysis was also performed utilizing tap water and industrial wastewater samples following the standard spiking method and 98.57% and 99% recoveries were obtained for tap water and industrial wastewater samples respectively. These findings undoubtedly indicate that the proposed sensor can be used in practical real-world applications.

K. Harikrishnan: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Gajendar Singh: Data curation, Methodology, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Amisha Kushwaha: Visualization. Varun Pratap Singh: Visualization. Umesh Kumar Gaur: Visualization, Validation.
